description:
A deployment engineer has requested that a new backend service, which listens on TCP port 8088, be made reachable through the host-based firewall rules stored in their personal project directory.

    Current set-up
    1. A plain-text iptables rule set already exists at:
         /home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables.rules
    2. The file is writable by the normal user.
       Its contents (before you begin) are exactly:

           *filter
           :INPUT DROP [0:0]
           :FORWARD DROP [0:0]
           :OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
           COMMIT

    3. A directory for change-tracking logs is present:
         /home/user/deployment/logs

    What you must achieve
    A. Add a single iptables rule that allows NEW inbound TCP traffic on port 8088.  
       • The rule MUST read exactly (no extra spaces, flags or comments):

           -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8088 -j ACCEPT

       • Position: it has to appear on its own line immediately above the
         literal line “COMMIT” inside /home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables.rules.

    B. Produce a text log confirming the change so that an automated audit
       system can pick it up later.  
       • Create a new file:
           /home/user/deployment/logs/firewall_update.log  
       • That file must contain precisely one line (terminated by a lone
         newline, no extra blank lines or spaces):

           Rule added: -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8088 -j ACCEPT

    Required output for automated grading
    1. The rule line must be present in the iptables rule file exactly as shown,
       and it must be right above the COMMIT line.
    2. The log file must exist with the single required line and nothing else.

    Constraints
    • You do NOT need sudo/root; all paths are inside your home directory.
    • The entire task can be completed with one straightforward shell command,
      but use whatever approach you find simplest.
    • Do not modify any other part of the file; preserve all existing lines
      exactly as they are.
    • Once finished, you may inspect the firewall file or cat the log to verify;
      console output is allowed.

    When you are done, the automated checker will:
        a) read /home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables.rules,
        b) confirm the new rule’s exact placement and spelling,
        c) read /home/user/deployment/logs/firewall_update.log,
        d) ensure the log’s contents match the specified single-line format.

    Tip: A single sed or printf >> redirection could accomplish the update,
    and creating the log file is just another redirection.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth for grading (hidden from agent) -->
    Initial file: /home/user/deployment/firewall/iptables.rules

    Content before task:

*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT

    Expected final content after agent completes:

*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8088 -j ACCEPT
COMMIT

    File to be created:
        /home/user/deployment/logs/firewall_update.log
    Expected exact content (including trailing newline, no extra spaces):

Rule added: -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8088 -j ACCEPT
name: task_000000_e685400d
